s1_b1: A stuck bolt. You push the wrench with everything you have. Nothing.
s1_b2: Then you slide a pipe over the handle to make it longer. Push gently. It turns.
s1_b3: Less force. More effect. Something other than force is doing the work here.
s1_b4: Push right at the bolt's center — and nothing turns, no matter how hard.
s1_b5: Push at the same spot but straight along the handle — also nothing. The direction matters too.
s1_b6: Turning depends on three things together: how hard, how far from the pivot, and which way.
s1_b7: Bundle them into one quantity. The turning effect of a force. Torque.
s1_b8: The distance that actually counts isn't the whole handle — it's the perpendicular distance from the pivot to where the force pushes.
s1_b9: That perpendicular reach has a name. The lever arm. Lengthen it, and the same force turns harder.
s1_b10: A door proves it daily. Push at the handle, it swings. Push next to the hinge, it fights you.
s1_b11: When several torques act, the rotation goes to whichever side wins the sum.
s1_b12: Computing the torque from a given force and distance — and finding equilibrium when multiple torques act — that's yours.
